message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this lever. Default refuted=true if uncertain. Check the artifacts at /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board and the code at /home/polaris/wt/flywheel.

LEVER: Narrated source-selection policy + structured abstract with X-not-Y thesis (L3)
RUBRIC CRITERION: "Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles'" (w=0.15) + "Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles" (w=0.10, both of instruction) + "S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization" (w=0.20 of readability)
CHANGE: cellcog's Scope/Methods/Source Selection paragraph survives the cleaner 100% and answers two instruction criteria in the instruction's own words — the cheapest surviving lever on the board. Emit deterministically from the ENRICHED bibliography: a 1.2 'Scope, Methods, and Source Selection' naming the actual journals in italics, stating 'restricted by design to peer-reviewed, English-language journal articles' (the word 'English' currently appears ZERO times in our report against a named w=0.10 criterion), declaring the grey-literature signpost convention, plus a 4-part **Objective/Methods/Findings/Contributions** abstract whose first 120 words carry a contrastive X-not-Y thesis (every winner does this; we open with a pipeline disclaimer). Template fill — zero fabrication IF and ONLY IF L1 landed.
WHERE: composer post-assembly — scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py after :745 (biblio available) before _maybe_reflow_report at :195; venue list generated from bibliography.json
CLAIMED: +0.008 to +0.012 overall (the two citation criteria are 6.25% of overall and we currently score them as a visible violator; the thesis/roadmap pays S1)
ADMITTED FAILURE: If retrieval is NOT actually journal-only, the paragraph is a checkable lie: our surviving grey-lit prose ('Goldman Sachs projects...', 'The World Economic Forum has predicted...') sits three sections below a claim of journal-only sourcing, and the judge reads both. Shipping L3 without L1 is worse than shipping nothing.
CONTRACT: Two-lane contract, zero-fabrication preserved by construction. LANE A (world-facts): unchanged — every sentence asserting a fact about the world carries [ev_XXX] tokens and passes verify_sentence_provenance (provenance_generator.py:2098) with the NEUTRAL clause (entailment_judge.py:588) intact. LANE B (adjudication): a sentence tagged [syn] by the writer is exempt from span entailment and admitted iff it introduces NO new fact — enforced by a new verify_synthesis_sentence beside the existing gate: (i) DETERMINISTIC closed-world check: every digit-bearing token, proper noun, and named mechanism in the [syn] sentence must already appear verbatim in this section's ADMITTED lane-A prose or the bibliography (cellcog's winning synthesis — 'The three frameworks are complementary rather than competitive... layered lenses to be deployed according to the question at hand' — passes this check by inspection: no new number, entity, or fact; only ranking/relating ideas already on the page); (ii) FAIL-CLOSED LLM check: 'does this sentence assert any empirical claim about the world not already asserted in the FACTS block above, or does it only rank, relate, reconcile, or evaluate them?' — any parse error, timeout, or uncertain verdict = drop the sentence. LANE B-NEG (negative-space claims): sentences of the form 'no peer-reviewed study in this corpus has yet X' are claims about the CORPUS, not the world, and are verified mechanically against the evidence pool + bibliography (they are also immune to the NEUTRAL clause by construction — they introduce no mechanism into any span). ATTRIBUTION: author/venue strings injected into prose must be byte-substrings of the ENRICHED sidecar record for the cited evidence_id (extending _audit_citations at compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:104), and the sidecar itself is spot-verified against source pages before any compose — the current sidecar fabricates venues ('Review of AI', wrong years) and MUST NOT be injected until repaired. Red-team acceptance: a 30-sentence set (15 genuine adjudications, 15 with one smuggled fact/number/entity each) must show >=12/15 genuine admitted and 0/15 smuggled admitted, else Lane B ships disabled and we fall back to lane-A-only compose.

ATTACK THROUGH THIS LENS:
FABRICATION: trace a concrete path by which an ungrounded fact, number, entity or attribution reaches the page. The moat is not for sale — a lever that leaks is unshippable at ANY score.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

import json,urllib.parse,re
b=json.load(open('outputs/rank11_final_compose/bibliography.json'))
JOURNALish=re.compile(r'(mdpi|sciencedirect|emerald|pmc\.ncbi|pubmed|doi\.org|ijtech|vilniustech|amegroups|rsisinternational|ijsra|elibrary\.imf|intereconomics|rtsa\.eu|springer|wiley|tandfonline|nature|oup|cambridge|sagepub)')
grey=[r for r in b if not JOURNALish.search(r['url'])]
print("NON-journal-domain records:", len(grey), "of", len(b))
for r in grey[:40]:
    print(f"  [{r['num']:>2}] {r['tier']} {urllib.parse.urlparse(r['url']).netloc:<38} {r['source_title'][:60]}")
EOF

content.content:

11:A reinstatement effect operates as the polar opposite of displacement: new labor-intensive tasks reinstate labor into a broader range of activities, directly increasing the labor share and labor demand.[7] Recent empirical research tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023 finds that AI's impact is often on specific tasks within jobs rather than on whole occupations, and when AI can perform most tasks comprising a job, the share of people in that role within a company falls by about 14%.[9] Conversely, when AI's impact is concentrated in just a few tasks within a role, employment in that role can grow, and even workers in high-wage roles heavily exposed to AI saw their share of total employment grow by about 3% over five years because AI boosted firm productivity.[9] One analysis using the US Census Bureau's Annual Business Survey finds that just 3.2% of firms used AI between 2016 and 2018, consistent with Eurostat data showing enterprise-level AI adoption ranging from 23% in Ireland to 3% in Hungary among OECD European Union countries.[10] Experimental evidence from a large-scale field experiment on an online labor market shows that employers offered AI-generated first drafts of job posts were 19% more likely to post a job and spent 44% less time writing posts, yet there was no discernible increase in matches, as marginal jobs were posted by employers with lower hiring intent and AI-generated posts were more generic and less informative.[14] A macroeconomic simulation suggests the existence of an AI-capital-to-labour ratio threshold beyond which even high rates of new job creation cannot prevent declines in per capita disposable income, with projections of a 26% decrease in per capita disposable income and a 21% decrease in the consumption index by mid-2050 absent a 10.8-fold increase in the new job creation rate.[15] A task-based framework within that same analysis decomposes production into distinct activities performed by either labor or capital, showing that if automation outpaces capital accumulation, it renders the two factors perfect substitutes on the margin.[16] One estimate cited in the literature places the growth effects of generative AI at only 0.07% per year over the coming decade, reflecting a pessimistic view that AI will not significantly advance beyond current capabilities.[17] A joint analysis by the Brookings Institution and the Stanford Digital Economy Lab finds that over 30 percent of US workers could see at least half of their job tasks affected by generative AI, with exposure concentrated in white-collar and entry-level occupations rather than in traditional manufacturing or manual labor.[18] The task-based framework treats production as a collection of tasks allocated between capital and labor, where automation shifts the task content of production against labor through a displacement effect while simultaneously generating a productivity effect that raises value added and thus labor demand in non-automated tasks.[7] The net impact of automation on labor demand depends on how the displacement and productivity effects weigh against each other.[7] Autor emphasizes that tasks which cannot be substituted by automation are generally complemented by it, drawing on the O-ring production function to illustrate how improvements in one set of tasks increase the economic value of the remaining tasks.[8] Historical evidence underscores this complementarity: the deployment of automated teller machines quadrupled from roughly 100,000 to 400,000 between 1995 and 2010, yet US bank teller employment rose modestly from 500,000 to approximately 550,000 over the 30-year period.[8] A large increase in AI use is linked to about 6% higher employment growth and 9.5% more sales growth over five years.[9] AI has made the most progress in automating non-routine, cognitive tasks, with the most progress made in the abilities "Information ordering", "Memorisation", and "Perceptual Speed".[10] The OECD finds that high-skill, white-collar occupations including business professionals, managers, chief executives, and science and engineering professionals are the most exposed to AI, while food preparation assistants, agricultural laborers, and cleaners are the least exposed.[10] The analysis formalizes scenarios in which automation raises the capital share substantially, while noting that if any technological progress is labor-augmenting, wages grow even under perfect substitution.[16] That same analysis reports that employment among workers aged 22-25 in highly AI-exposed occupations has declined relative to older cohorts, suggesting that firms are beginning to substitute AI for junior labor in tasks that historically served as entry points into professional careers.[18] The task-based framework's distinction between displacement and productivity effects provides a lens for interpreting AI's labor market impact, and empirical evidence to date suggests that displacement effects have been modest relative to theoretical exposure levels[7][10][9][11][13]
20:Their analysis demonstrates robust negative effects of robots on employment and wages across commuting zones, with areas most exposed to robots after 1990 showing no differential pre-trends, confirming that robots' impact is distinct from other capital and technologies.[29] By 2023, the International Federation of Robotics reported that average robot density in manufacturing worldwide reached a new high of 141 robots per 10,000 employees, underscoring the continuing relevance of these displacement dynamics.[30] One analysis suggests that for every 1% expansion in the robot workforce, approximately 4.6% of jobs face the prospect of replacement, with 76% of workers potentially susceptible over the next two decades.[31] Displacing AI innovations were found to lower operating costs, while augmenting AI innovations raised firm-level productivity, indicating that the valuation effects depend critically on external labor market frictions.[32] Notably, the evidence does not support the hypothesis that high-quality service moderates these adverse effects; in fact, there is suggestive evidence that top freelancers are disproportionately affected, with a 1% increase in prior earnings associated with an additional 0.5% reduction in jobs and 1.7% reduction in monthly income for text-based services.[33] Cross-country evidence from 23 OECD countries extends these findings to an international context, revealing that overall there appears to be no clear relationship between AI exposure and employment growth across occupations.[34] Average AI exposure varies little across countries—from 0.52 in Lithuania to 0.72 in Finland—but varies substantially across occupations, from 0.26 for cleaners and helpers to 0.87 for business professionals.[34] The transport sector offers a concrete case of large-scale projected displacement: without driverless trucks, approximately 6.4 million truck drivers are projected to be needed across Europe and the United States by 2030, yet fewer than 5.6 million are projected to be available and willing to work.[35] At the macroeconomic level, the Penn Wharton Budget Model estimates that 40% of current GDP could be substantially affected by generative AI, with occupations around the 80th percentile of earnings being the most exposed.[36] Suggestive evidence indicates that AI adoption is already affecting the labor market: for jobs that can be performed entirely by generative AI, employment was 0.75% lower in 2024 than in 2021, and employment growth has slowed significantly since 2022 in occupations with high AI exposure.[36] Across OECD countries more broadly, approximately 9% of all jobs are at high risk of automation and an additional 25% face high risk of substantial change, meaning more than one-third of all jobs are potentially vulnerable.[37] In India, AI and automation are projected to displace more than 69% of jobs, while in the UK the wholesale and retail sector, which holds the largest employment share at 14.8%, is expected to be significantly impacted by automation.[38] Acemoglu and Restrepo study the impact of industrial robots on US labor markets and find that one more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42%.[29] The average firm in this sample held 7.07 AI patents annually, with 4.84 classified as augmenting and 2.21 as displacing.[32] These effects operate on both the extensive and intensive margins—freelancers were 1.2% less likely to receive any employment in a given month and took 4.7% fewer jobs conditional on employment—and are generally at least as large as the effects of other technological and non-technological labor market shocks.[33] Image-based generative AI produced qualitatively similar results, with a 2.1% reduction in monthly jobs and a 5.2% decrease in total monthly earnings.[33] Employment grew by 10.8% on average across all occupations and countries between 2012 and 2019, yet average usual weekly hours declined by 0.40%.[34] The share of job postings requiring AI skills remained very low overall, rising from 0.10% in 2012 to 0.24% in 2019.[34] If driverless trucks are deployed quickly, between 3.4 and 4.4 million of these driver jobs would become redundant, and even accounting for demographic trends, driver displacement could result.[35]
24:In that experiment, the treatment group's time on the post-treatment task dropped by 11 minutes, representing 0.75 standard deviations relative to the control group, which took an average of 27 minutes.[39] Average evaluator grades in the treatment group increased by 0.45 standard deviations, with similar increases for overall grades and specific grades for writing quality, content quality, and originality.[39] Notably, ChatGPT reduced productivity inequality: the correlation between first-task and second-task grades fell from 0.41 in the control group to 0.14 in the treatment group, driven by the fact that participants who scored lower on the first task benefited more from ChatGPT access.[39] Workers exposed to ChatGPT during the experiment were 2 times as likely to report using it in their real job 2 weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely 2 months after.[40][39] Field experiments at a leading cross-border online retail platform over six months in 2023-2024 found that GenAI adoption significantly increased sales, with treatment effects ranging from 0% to 16.3% depending on GenAI's marginal contribution relative to existing firm practices.[41] Because inputs and prices were held constant across experimental arms, these gains mapped directly into total factor productivity improvements, with the implied annual incremental value approximately $5 per consumer across the four applications with positive effects.[41] The primary mechanism operated through higher conversion rates, consistent with GenAI reducing frictions in the marketplace, and smaller and newer sellers as well as less experienced consumers exhibited disproportionately larger gains.[41] One survey of more than 100,000 workers from 11 GenAI-exposed occupations found that workers estimated ChatGPT could reduce working times by 50% for one-third of their job tasks.[42] Investments in GenAI were projected to grow by 60% over the following three years, reaching 7.6% of IT budgets by 2027.[42] Generative AI has the potential to contribute between $2.6 trillion and $4.4 trillion annually to the global economy, with around three-fourths of this added value concentrated in customer operations, marketing, and related areas.[43][45] (also mirrored) The Wharton PWBM estimated that AI will increase productivity and GDP by 1.5% by 2035, nearly 3% by 2055, and 3.7% by 2075.[46][47] AI's boost to annual productivity growth was projected to peak at 0.2 percentage points in 2032, with a permanent effect of less than 0.04 percentage points due to sectoral shifts.[46][47] Occupations around the 80th percentile of earnings were the most exposed, with around half of their work susceptible to automation by AI, while the highest-earning and lowest-earning occupations were less exposed.[46][47] By the second half of 2024, 26.4% of workers used generative AI at work, while 33.7% of adults used it outside of work, with early adoption patterns broadly similar to personal computer adoption in the early 1980s.[46][47] BCG estimated that generative AI, once implemented at scale in customer service operations, could increase productivity by 30% to 50%, and 95% of global customer service leaders expected customers to be served by AI bots within three years.[48] In customer service settings, a generative AI assistant was associated with a 14% increase in task completion rate, while job interviews conducted with a generative AI voice agent produced a 17% increase in job starts and an 18% increase in retention rate.[47] GitHub Copilot was associated with a 56% increase in speed for JavaScript programming tasks and a 26% increase in task completion rate for software development.[47] A survey of 366 employees found that both cognitive and social uses of GenAI tools significantly enhanced innovative performance, with cognitive use facilitating knowledge transfer and social use bolstering resource acquisition.[49] Enhanced knowledge transfer and resource acquisition improved job satisfaction, which was pivotal in driving innovative performance among GenAI users.[49] Among 148 university graduates surveyed, those who reported greater knowledge and more frequent use of AI tools, especially generative ones, were more likely to be employed in areas related to their majors and to perceive higher productivity.[50] Firm-level studies found positive employment effects of industrial robot use, with automating firms having between 2% and 10% higher employment, and positive elasticity of employment to robot investment between 0.2% and 2%.[51] Following the release of ChatGPT, a portfolio investing in firms with high exposure to generative AI and shorting those with low exposure yielded 0.45% daily returns, suggesting investors generally perceived ChatGPT's release as valuable.[52] One multidisciplinary article bringing together 43 contributions from experts acknowledged ChatGPT's capabilities to enhance productivity and suggested significant gains in banking, hospitality, tourism, and information technology industries.[53] A World Economic Forum report distinguishes between job automation and job augmentation.[42]
28:The OECD has taken stock of what is known about AI's impact on the labour market, including effects on employment and wages, the transformation of jobs and skill needs, and changes to the work environment.[54] Their evidence indicates that, on average, occupations impacted by AI experience a small but positive change in wages, with no change in employment.[55] These findings suggest that access to complementary skills and technologies plays an important role in determining AI's impact, and that AI has the potential to exacerbate labor market polarization.[55] A systematic literature review confirms that AI's impact on employment is multifaceted, with new opportunities arising from the emergence of high-skilled jobs and rising demand for interdisciplinary talent, alongside challenges from the replacement of low-skilled jobs and polarization of the employment structure.[56] The demand for skills driven by AI is moving towards higher levels of expertise and multidisciplinary integration.[56] The Congressional Budget Office notes that AI has the potential to change how businesses and the federal government provide goods and services, which could affect economic growth, employment and wages, and the distribution of income in the economy.[57] Research on generative AI's labor market effects finds that, unlike past mechanization that replaced physical labor, GenAI has the greatest impact on high-skilled, professional work—the kinds of roles that define the 21st-century knowledge economy and that have long been considered safe havens from roboticization.[58][59] Among occupational sectors ranked by GenAI exposure score, mortgage and nonmortgage loan brokers scored highest at 3.909, with the report also identifying law offices, investment banking, and commercial banking among the most exposed industries.[58] Morgan analysts estimated that interest in AI models had driven $1.4 trillion in increased market capitalization and a 45% increase in corporate profits across the S&P 500.[58] Their analysis also projects that between 2024 and 2035, employment in construction in the US will increase by 4.4%, and for architects and engineers by 6.8%.[60] Female students were found to be in training that leads to greater AI exposure in jobs, with an overall exposure score of 0.34 compared to 0.06 for males.[61] Among higher education subjects, economics at Level 7+ had the highest AIOE at 1.26, followed by mathematics and statistics at 1.15, while animal care and veterinary science at Level 6 had the lowest at -0.03.[61] The Cleveland Federal Reserve documented that through 2015, expensive metropolitan areas saw a 6.5 percentage point decline in the share of clerical jobs, compared to just a 2.3 percentage point decline in affordable areas.[62] The Bipartisan Policy Center reports that there is currently no evidence of widespread job loss caused by generative AI, even in jobs that have seen the greatest use of AI, because AI tends to impact specific tasks within jobs rather than entire jobs.[63] In developing countries, informal employment represents 89% of total employment in low-income countries compared to 15.9% in high-income countries, and just over 40% of populations in low- and middle-income countries were connected to the internet in 2020 compared to 75% in high-income countries.[64] Researchers identify 7.2 million workers, representing 5.3% of the US workforce, who combine high AI exposure with low adaptive capacity, concentrated in clerical and administrative support roles, suggesting that exposure measures alone may obscure important heterogeneity in workers' vulnerability to AI-driven disruption.[64] Business students generally perceive AI positively, recognizing its potential to increase organizational efficiency and work productivity, though findings reveal a dual impact of AI on society, organizations, and the labor market.[65] The adoption of artificial intelligence has prompted inquiry into its potential effects on employment and wages.[57][54] The positive wage correlation is driven primarily by occupations with higher software skill requirements.[55] Research estimates that if current AI use cases were expanded across the economy, 2.5% of US employment would be at risk of related job loss, with unemployment increasing by half a percentage point during the AI transition period.[60] The analysis assessed exposure using the AIOE methodology, finding that early-career employees with higher levels of qualification are typically in occupations more exposed to AI.[61] Research using the Current Population Survey shows at most small changes in hiring in AI-exposed jobs.[66]
36:An alternative estimate places the share of jobs exposed to full automation at 7%, reinforcing the view that AI is transforming jobs more than eliminating them.[72] (also mirrored) This study draws on 285 million job postings from Lightcast, aggregated into a balanced panel of 6.8 million state–industry–occupation–quarter cells, spanning 2018Q1 to 2025Q2.[74] (also mirrored) The baseline difference-in-differences estimates show that the high-substitution occupation effect intensifies from -0.064 in the first post year to -0.182 by the third year under the most restrictive specification.[74] (also mirrored) The authors note that for comparison, Acemoglu and Restrepo found that one additional robot per thousand workers reduced the employment-to-population ratio by only 0.18–0.34 percentage points in the U.S. between 1990 and 2007, while Acemoglu et al. reported no discernible impact of earlier AI exposure on employment or wages during 2010–2018.[74] (also mirrored) Goldman Sachs forecasts a 7% increase in global GDP over a ten-year period, equivalent to annual growth of 0.67%.[77] In contrast, Acemoglu et al. estimate that AI's contribution to total factor productivity gains may not rise above 0.7 percentage points over ten years, even assuming full automation of exposed tasks, with GDP impact remaining modest at 1.1%.[72] (also mirrored) The IMF simulations by Cerutti et al. illustrate two contrasting trajectories: in a rapid adoption scenario, global TFP grows by 2.4% in ten years and GDP increases by 4%, while in a slower scenario GDP growth is limited to approximately 1.3% with TFP growing by only 1.8%.[72] (also mirrored) The benefits of AI appear to be concentrated in advanced countries and the services sector, accentuating differences between regions and levels of technological development.[72] (also mirrored) In the optimistic ten-year scenario, GDP would increase by 5.4% in the United States and 4.4% in Europe, compared with just 3% on average in emerging economies and 2.2% in low-income countries.[72] (also mirrored) The IMF's Gen-AI report further documents substantial heterogeneity behind average figures: in advanced economies, the share of employment in high-exposure, high-complementarity occupations ranges between 20.2 and 37.3%, while in low-income countries the low-exposure share ranges from 54 to 96.1%.[78] The literature emphasises that current technologies rarely automate an entire job and that it is primarily specific tasks that can be automated[72] (also mirrored) 3.3% of global employment falls into the highest exposure category, while one in four workers globally are in occupations with some generative AI exposure.[79]

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/wt/flywheel && python3 - <<'EOF'
import json
b=json.load(open('outputs/rank11_final_compose/bibliography.json'))
for r in b:
    if r.get('venue'):
        print(f"[{r['num']:>2}] venue={r['venue']!r:<45} year={r.get('year')} url={r['url'][:65]}")
EOF
